correct_explanation: Client-side JavaScript is fully visible to any user via browser devtools, source inspection, or network tab—embedding an API key there is equivalent to publishing it publicly. Any third party can extract the key and make authenticated requests on your account. API keys must live exclusively on a server-side layer that the browser never directly sees.

distractor_explanations.A: Spending caps mitigate the financial damage after a key is compromised but do not prevent the key extraction itself—the root cause. This is a recovery control, not a prevention of the architectural exposure.
distractor_explanations.C: The Anthropic API does not offer domain-scoped or origin-restricted API keys; this feature does not exist. Even if it did, a key visible in a client bundle is already extracted before any origin check occurs.
distractor_explanations.D: Modern browsers enforce HTTPS for most API endpoints, and the scenario gives no indication HTTP was used. The key was extracted from the bundle itself, not intercepted in transit—HTTPS is irrelevant to static asset inspection.

correct_explanation: Formatting variance is a prompt specification problem: Claude defaults to context-appropriate formatting when no constraints are given, producing inconsistent output across varied inputs. Adding explicit format instructions to the system prompt enforces a consistent output contract on every request, regardless of ticket content. This is the correct intervention because it controls structure at the generation stage, before output reaches the rendering layer.

distractor_explanations.A: Streaming controls token delivery order to the client, not output structure or length — the model still generates the same variable-format content, just incrementally. Stopping consumption mid-stream truncates output arbitrarily rather than enforcing a format.
distractor_explanations.B: Capping `max_tokens` truncates responses at a token count but does not enforce plain-text format, required sections, or consistent structure — a response can still be markdown-formatted and structurally inconsistent within the cap.
distractor_explanations.D: Passing a prior response as an assistant turn creates an implicit formatting hint but provides no guaranteed contract — Claude may still diverge in structure across different ticket inputs, especially as content complexity changes.

correct_explanation: Explicit negative constraints directly address the root cause: Claude's default behavior is to provide helpful context, so without instructions to suppress it, elaboration is expected. Specifying that the consumer is an automated pipeline further signals that conversational output is inappropriate, reinforcing the format constraint. This eliminates ambiguity in the instruction set rather than patching around it downstream.

distractor_explanations.A: This is a brittle workaround that treats the symptom, not the cause — if Claude's verbose output changes structure or uses synonyms, the regex breaks silently. The moderation decision can still be dropped or misclassified without fixing the prompt.
distractor_explanations.C: Temperature controls sampling randomness, not instruction-following behavior. Verbosity here is driven by Claude defaulting to a helpful explanatory register, which persists across temperature settings.
distractor_explanations.D: Instruction-following models handle single-token output tasks correctly when given explicit format constraints — the failure is a prompt engineering gap, not an architectural limitation. Replacing the model discards the system's flexibility without addressing the actual problem.

correct_explanation: Claude does not reliably infer format constraints from role assignment alone — explicit negative constraints (no explanations, automated audience) must be stated directly to suppress verbose output in a zero-tolerance pipeline. A classifier role tells Claude what to do, but without explicit prohibitions, it retains latitude to add context it judges helpful. Separating role/taxonomy definition from format enforcement is the failure point this architecture must close.

distractor_explanations.A: The failure mode here is conflating persistence with sufficiency — a system prompt persists but only enforces what it explicitly states. Role-framing alone does not carry implicit format constraints, so omitting explicit prohibitions leaves behavioral variance in the output.
distractor_explanations.C: Structured output parameters constrain schema shape, not natural-language verbosity within a field. Mixing prompt-based and API-level controls is not conflicting — they operate on different layers — and dropping prompt constraints leaves format behavior underspecified.
distractor_explanations.D: System prompt length does not correlate with hallucinated labels; taxonomy enumeration in the prompt actually reduces label drift by anchoring Claude to the defined set. Keeping the prompt artificially short to avoid hallucination sacrifices the constraint enforcement the pipeline depends on.

correct_explanation: Progressive summarization addresses the root cause directly: it compresses information density for older turns rather than discarding them, preserving the original complaint and key dispute facts across arbitrarily long sessions. The running summary holds critical continuity (what the user originally reported) while full message fidelity for recent turns maintains conversational coherence. Token consumption grows logarithmically rather than linearly, satisfying the cost constraint.

distractor_explanations.A: Upgrading to a larger context model is cost-additive for every session, not just the 23% that exceed 40 turns, violating the cost constraint. It also defers rather than solves the problem — sufficiently long sessions will still exhaust even a larger window.
distractor_explanations.C: Raising the truncation threshold from 15 to 30 retained turns delays the failure point without eliminating it; sessions that run 60+ turns will reproduce the exact same re-explanation problem. This is a threshold adjustment, not an architectural fix to unbounded context growth.
distractor_explanations.D: Semantic retrieval fetches turns similar to the current message, but billing disputes require chronological narrative continuity — the original complaint may be semantically distant from the current negotiation step and never retrieved. This approach trades structural context coherence for keyword relevance, introducing new failure modes rather than fixing the existing one.

correct_explanation: Summarization-before-eviction preserves semantic continuity by converting discarded turns into a persistent context artifact that travels forward in the conversation window. The summary sits at index 0 as a stable reference, so critical facts like the account number remain accessible regardless of how many turns have been pruned. This is the standard architectural pattern for unbounded multi-turn sessions: compress what you lose rather than silently dropping it.

distractor_explanations.B: Token-counting improves efficiency but is purely a deferral strategy — it delays the problem rather than solving it. Once the 80% threshold triggers truncation, the same critical-context loss at turn 3 still occurs.
distractor_explanations.C: Storing history in a database preserves data durability but provides zero benefit to the model, which only sees the 20-turn slice passed to the API. The model cannot infer an account number it was never given in the current request.
distractor_explanations.D: max_tokens controls the length of the model's output, not the size of the input context window. Increasing it has no effect on how many tokens the request can contain and does not address context overflow.

correct_explanation: Any secret embedded in a client-side JavaScript bundle is effectively public: users can read it via DevTools, network inspection, or source extraction in seconds. An exposed API key grants the finder full, unrestricted access to the team's Anthropic account—running up costs, exhausting rate limits, or exfiltrating data. Keeping the key in a server-side environment variable (as the lab's `os.environ` pattern demonstrates) means the credential never leaves infrastructure the team controls.

distractor_explanations.A: The `system` parameter is a standard JSON field in the request body, and browsers can set arbitrary JSON fields in a `fetch()` POST request. There is no API restriction that blocks `system` from client-side calls.
distractor_explanations.C: Server-side proxies do not write to the browser's local storage—that is a client-side browser API entirely unrelated to where the proxy runs. Caching Claude responses to reduce token costs would require a server-side cache (e.g., Redis), not local storage.
distractor_explanations.D: The Anthropic API does not unconditionally block CORS; CORS behavior depends on the server's response headers, not an absolute prohibition on browser requests. While CORS can be a practical hurdle, it is configurable and is not the core security justification for the proxy pattern.

correct_explanation: System prompt constraints are applied universally on every request, making them the correct architectural layer for enforcing output contracts that downstream systems depend on. By specifying word count, plain-text format, required sections, and audience context in the system prompt, the model produces compliant output before post-processing is ever needed. This eliminates the root cause — variable output structure — rather than patching symptoms downstream.

distractor_explanations.A: Placing format constraints in the user-turn prompt introduces per-request variability and requires every caller to correctly replicate the constraints — a fragile contract that breaks when tickets are submitted without them. System-level invariants belong in the system prompt, not the user turn.
distractor_explanations.C: Temperature controls output randomness and creativity, not structural formatting or length. Lowering temperature does not prevent Claude from generating markdown or long responses when the prompt provides no explicit format constraints.
distractor_explanations.D: Sentence-boundary truncation improves coherence at the cut point but leaves the root cause — unconstrained variable-length markdown output — completely intact. Executives still receive summaries of arbitrary length and structure; the fix only makes the overflow slightly less jarring.
